model_narrative: We lay out an investment and production plan for a small economy over a sequence of periods. In each period we decide how much each sector produces, how much it imports and exports, how much new capacity of each type to build and whether to open each unit, how much debt to carry and how much foreign exchange to convert into domestic resources, and how large the consumption increment is. We also set terminal valuations that credit the capacity and savings left standing at the end. The objective is to maximize total welfare, the discounted stream of consumption increments plus the terminal value.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the producing sectors of the economy

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the capacity types a sector can install, a normal unit and an auxiliary unit

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the two scarce resource accounts that every activity draws on, a foreign-exchange account and a domestic-savings account

sets.name: te
sets.doc: the extended time horizon in chronological order; the first member is the base period before any decisions, the middle members are the operating periods, and the last member is the terminal period that closes the plan

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the operating periods in chronological order, that is the extended horizon with the base period removed

sets.name: ia
sets.doc: the sectors that require auxiliary capacity units

sets.name: im
sets.doc: the sectors whose output may be imported

sets.name: ie
sets.doc: the sectors whose output may be exported

sets.name: in_
sets.doc: the set of valid sector-and-capacity-type pairs, that is which capacity types actually exist for each sector; pairs outside this set carry no capacity and are skipped

params.name: a
params.doc: the input-output matrix; a[i,j] is the amount of sector i's output consumed to make one unit of sector j's output, so a column of a describes one sector's recipe

params.name: u
params.doc: the amount of resource r used per unit of output of sector i; the foreign column is the import content and the domestic column is the domestic-input content

params.name: rbase
params.doc: the base-period endowment available in each resource account, in the model's value units

params.name: alpha
params.doc: each sector's fixed share in the consumption bundle, so consumption of sector i grows in proportion to alpha[i]

params.name: cbb
params.doc: the fixed base level of final demand for each sector that must be met every period independent of the consumption increment

params.name: kb
params.doc: the base-period installed capacity of each sector-and-type pair; the normal-type entry is also used as the reference stock when valuing terminal capacity

params.name: delt
params.doc: the welfare discount factor applied to each operating period, a geometric weight that falls toward later periods

params.name: inv_fixed
params.doc: the lump fixed charge in resource r incurred whenever a sector-and-type unit is opened in a period, charged once per opening regardless of size; nonzero only for the units that enjoy economies of scale

params.name: inv_prop
params.doc: the per-unit-of-expansion charge in resource r for adding capacity to a sector-and-type pair, applied to the amount of expansion

params.name: rho
params.doc: the per-period discount rate used to build the welfare weights and to roll debt forward, equal to 0.07

params.name: vmax
params.doc: the largest expansion that a single opening of an economies-of-scale unit may carry, equal to 200; it links the size of an expansion to the open indicator

params.name: cmin
params.doc: the minimum required period-over-period rise in the consumption increment, equal to 10.6

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the output produced by each sector in each period

vars.name: m
vars.doc: the quantity of each sector's good imported in each period; meaningful only for importable sectors

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the quantity of each sector's good exported in each period; meaningful only for exportable sectors

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the capacity expansion (amount added) for a sector-and-type pair in a period; the addition becomes available the following period

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the open indicator for a sector-and-type pair in a period, one if the unit is opened that period and zero otherwise; used to trigger the fixed charge and to cap the expansion size

vars.name: k
vars.doc: the capacity stock on hand for a sector-and-type pair at the start of each period; its base-period value is fixed to the base capacity

vars.name: ke3
vars.doc: the leftover capacity of a sector-and-type pair credited to the first block of post-terminal years

vars.name: ke8
vars.doc: the leftover capacity of a sector-and-type pair credited to the second block of post-terminal years

vars.name: c
vars.doc: the consumption increment in each operating period, the welfare-bearing quantity the plan tries to raise

vars.name: d
vars.doc: the level of outstanding foreign debt carried into each period, capped above by a debt ceiling

vars.name: ufe
vars.doc: unused foreign exchange carried from one period into the next

vars.name: ms
vars.doc: foreign exchange converted into domestic resources in a period; one unit drawn from the foreign account yields 0.8 of a unit in the domestic account

vars.name: vr
vars.doc: the terminal savings stock that funds resource use beyond the horizon

vars.name: vc3
vars.doc: the valuation credit for leftover capacity over the first post-terminal block

vars.name: vc8
vars.doc: the valuation credit for leftover capacity over the second post-terminal block

vars.name: vc
vars.doc: the valuation of capacity still in productive use at the terminal period

vars.name: wterm
vars.doc: the terminal value, the discounted worth assigned to everything left standing at the end of the horizon

vars.name: welfare
vars.doc: the total discounted welfare, the objective quantity

constraints.name: im);
constraints.intent: Each sector's output, after netting out what is consumed as inputs by every sector, together with any imports of that sector's good, must cover that sector's needs in every operating period. For each sector and operating period, the good produced net of its use as an input across all sectors, plus imports where importing is allowed, equals exports where exporting is allowed plus the sector's share of the consumption increment plus its fixed base demand.

constraints.name: in_)
constraints.intent: A sector cannot produce more in a period than the capacity it has available. For each sector, capacity type, and operating period where that capacity type exists for the sector, output may not exceed the capacity stock on hand.

constraints.name: in_)
constraints.intent: At the end of the plan, the capacity that a sector-and-type pair has beyond what it is still producing is accounted for as leftover capacity split across the two post-terminal blocks. For each valid sector and capacity type, the leftover credited to the first block plus the leftover credited to the second block equals the terminal capacity stock minus the terminal output of that sector.

constraints.name: in_)
constraints.intent: Capacity carries forward period to period, growing only by what was added the period before. For each valid sector and capacity type and for every period after the base period, the capacity stock at the start of the period equals the stock at the start of the previous period plus the expansion built in the previous period.

constraints.name: i:
constraints.intent: For the capacity units that carry a fixed opening charge, building expansion is only allowed when the unit is opened, and the size of any single opening is capped. For each such sector and capacity type and for every period after the base period, the expansion built in the previous period may not exceed the largest allowed opening size times the open indicator for that previous period, so no expansion happens unless the unit is opened and even then it is bounded.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Each resource account must stay within what is available in every period, balancing all the demands on it against its sources. For each resource and each period in the extended horizon, add up the resource used by production, the foreign account's net import bill, the fixed and proportional costs of opening and expanding capacity, debt rolled forward with interest net of new debt taken on, foreign exchange converted into the domestic account, carried foreign exchange, and the terminal savings draw, and require the total not to exceed the account's base endowment, which is available only in the base period. Production and trade terms apply only in the operating periods, investment and new-debt and carried-exchange terms apply only in the non-terminal periods, and the terminal savings term applies only in the terminal period. Converting one unit of foreign exchange yields eight tenths of a unit in the domestic account.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: The terminal valuation credited to leftover capacity may not exceed the value of the capacity actually left over. For each resource, the leftover capacity across all valid sector-and-type pairs, weighted by each pair's resource intensity, must be at least the resource intensity of the leftover capacity stock times the combined first-block and second-block valuation credits.

constraints.name: t)
constraints.intent: Consumption must rise by at least a fixed floor from one operating period to the next. For every operating period after the first, the consumption increment must be at least the previous period's increment plus the minimum required rise.

constraints.name: t)
constraints.intent: Primary-sector output is not allowed to fall over time. For every operating period after the first, the primary sector's output must be at least its output in the previous period.

constraints.name: te)[-1]
constraints.intent: The valuation placed on capacity still in productive use at the end of the plan is limited by how much terminal output exceeds the base reference capacity. For each sector, the input-output-weighted excess of terminal output over base normal capacity across all sectors must be at least that sector's consumption share times the terminal capacity valuation.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: The terminal value gathers up the discounted worth of everything left standing at the end of the horizon. Set the terminal value equal to the terminal savings and the terminal capacity-in-use valuation each weighted by one long-run discount factor, plus the first-block leftover valuation and the second-block leftover valuation each weighted by its own further-discounted factor.

constraints.name: welfare
constraints.intent: Total welfare collects the discounted stream of consumption together with the end-of-plan value. Set total welfare equal to the sum over all operating periods of the period's discount weight times its consumption increment, plus the terminal value.

query: Let's try capping total finished-goods imports across the whole horizon at 30 units. Run it and let me see the welfare impact.
expected_pyomo: model.wf_cap_imp_fin = Constraint(expr=sum(model.m[te, 'finished'] for te in model.te) <= 30)

query: The plan ships in a mountain of intermediate goods from abroad. Shouldn't we rein that in — say, hold total intermediate imports under 100? What's the trade-off that has the optimizer importing so heavily?
expected_pyomo: model.want_cap_imp_int = Constraint(expr=sum(model.m[te, 'intermed'] for te in model.te) <= 100)
